explanation.text: Little Shaq says he is responsible, but his actions show that he forgets the trash and needs daily reminders to make his bed. This contrast supports judging responsibility by what someone does, not what someone says.
Little Shaq's favorite spaghetti and meatballs remain untouched because he is worried about asking for a kitten. Being too nervous to eat food he loves is the clearest supporting detail.

“Glanced” means looked briefly. Little Shaq looks briefly around the room while seeking something that might help, so choice A, “looked for something,” is the closest option in context even though it describes his purpose rather than the exact meaning of the word.

Little Shaq offers to complete the many Star of the Week jobs and care for Flopsy to prove he can handle a kitten. His parents then agree to consider his request, showing that responsible work may be rewarded.
Little Shaq smiles and calls his parents' response “a start” because, although they have not promised a kitten, their agreement to think about it makes him happy that his request is now possible.
Caring for the class rabbit is Little Shaq's main chance to prove he can accept pet responsibilities and persuade his parents. Therefore, his plan to care for Flopsy is essential to a summary.
The mountain lion tries to attack Porcupette but receives sharp quills in its mouth as a result. Its slinking away in pain therefore shows an effect caused by Porcupette's defense.
Paragraph 2 describes Porcupette waddling on short, sturdy legs while calm and confident. The picture therefore supplies the visual details of a baby porcupine's legs, shape, fur, and quills.
Porcupette has spent the night climbing, swimming, finding food, and escaping a mountain lion, then dreams of still more adventures. Therefore, her “prickly porcupine world” is full of excitement.
Without waking her mother, Porcupette finds food, climbs, swims, warns an owl, defeats a mountain lion, and finds shelter for sleep. These successful actions show that she can survive independently.

Porcupette raises her quills to warn away an owl and later drives a mountain lion off with quills stuck in its mouth. Because both dangers are stopped this way, quills keeping porcupines safe is a central idea.

The passage follows one night in sequence: Porcupette leaves her den, eats and climbs, swims and explores, escapes a predator, then sleeps as morning arrives. Therefore, time order is the main organization.
A raccoon passes Porcupette on its way to fish, and she remains calm, confident, and unbothered. Their peaceful encounter shows that different animals can share the same nighttime habitat.

Jamie repeatedly organizes others and worries whether their campaign will work because she believes they must save the zoo. Her statement “We have to save the zoo” therefore expresses the story's central goal.
Jamie cannot vote, so she organizes classmates to make signs, dozens of posters, and fliers displayed around town. Spreading those messages is how the children persuade eligible voters.
Jamie reads that the zoo lost, immediately worries about the animals, and wishes the class had campaigned longer. Those disappointed thoughts show that “her heart sank” means she became sad.
Jamie's mother points out that the animals will move to the nearby Heritage Wildlife Preserve and have plenty of space. This reassurance shows her belief that they will be safe and well there.

At first Jamie eagerly creates a campaign to keep the zoo open. Although the vote fails, she accepts that the animals “won” because they will receive more space at a preserve, showing gratitude for the good outcome.

Vikram stays at the cottage to take pictures and later explains that he was photographing the beach while dismissing the shell as boring. Together, these details show more interest in photography than in Sabeel's treasure.

Sabeel loves the shell but realizes it is Sandy's home, not a possession to keep, and says he must “return a gift.” His choice supports a theme of placing a living creature's needs above one's own wants.
After finding the shell under Vikram's bed, Sabeel immediately declares, “Vikram took it.” He therefore makes the claim that his brother stole the shell, even though Vikram denies it.
When Sabeel picks up the hermit crab, its legs and eyes withdraw inside the shell. Pulling its exposed body into its protective covering shows that the crab senses possible danger.
Sabeel remembers the shell arriving from the ocean with a wave. Because moving seawater brought it onto shore, “washed up” means carried by flowing water.

Dadi first calls the empty-looking shell a gift from the sea. Once it is known to house a crab, she reminds Sabeel that Sandy already has a home, therefore guiding him to return the living animal to the ocean.
